title: Owls Can Turn Their Heads 270 Degrees
content: Owls cannot move their eyes like humans because their eyes are fixed in their sockets. Instead, they rotate their heads up to 270 degrees, giving them an impressive field of view while hunting.

title: Masters of Night Vision
content: Owls have large eyes specially designed to gather light, allowing them to see exceptionally well in low-light conditions. This makes them highly effective nighttime hunters.

title: Silent Flight Experts
content: The edges of an owl's feathers are specially adapted to reduce noise. Their nearly silent flight allows them to sneak up on prey without being heard.

title: More Than 250 Species
content: There are over 250 owl species living around the world. They can be found on every continent except Antarctica, adapting to forests, deserts, mountains, and grasslands.

title: Excellent Hearing
content: Many owls have unevenly placed ears that help them detect the exact location of prey, even when it is hidden beneath snow or leaves.

title: Most Owls Hunt at Night
content: Many owl species are nocturnal, meaning they are most active after sunset. Darkness gives them an advantage over many of their prey.

title: Not All Owls Are Nocturnal
content: Some owl species prefer hunting during the day or at dawn and dusk. Their activity depends on their habitat and food sources.

title: Owls Are Carnivores
content: Owls feed on mice, rats, birds, insects, reptiles, frogs, and even fish. Their diet varies depending on the species and where they live.

title: They Swallow Prey Whole
content: Small prey is often swallowed whole. Later, owls regurgitate pellets containing bones, fur, feathers, and other indigestible materials.

title: Pellets Help Scientists
content: Scientists study owl pellets to learn about local ecosystems. The bones inside reveal what owls have been eating and what animals live nearby.

title: Baby Owls Are Called Owlets
content: Young owls are known as owlets. They remain in the nest until their feathers develop enough for them to fly and hunt on their own.

title: Females Are Often Larger
content: In many owl species, females are slightly larger and heavier than males. This size difference may help during nesting and protecting young.

title: Owls Rarely Build Their Own Nests
content: Instead of building nests, many owls occupy abandoned nests, tree cavities, rocky cliffs, or old buildings.

title: Powerful Talons
content: Owls have incredibly strong feet and razor-sharp talons that allow them to catch, grip, and carry prey with ease.

title: Perfect Natural Camouflage
content: The patterns on an owl's feathers blend perfectly with tree bark and branches, making them difficult for predators and prey to notice.

title: Owls Speak Through Calls
content: Owls use hoots, whistles, screeches, and clicks to communicate with mates, warn rivals, and defend their territory.

title: The Smallest Owl
content: The Elf Owl is the smallest owl in the world. It stands only about 13 to 15 centimeters tall and weighs around 40 grams.

title: The Largest Owl
content: Blakiston's Fish Owl is one of the largest owl species on Earth, with a wingspan that can reach nearly 2 meters.

title: Symbols of Wisdom
content: For centuries, owls have symbolized wisdom, knowledge, and mystery in many cultures. In some traditions, they are also considered protectors or spiritual guides.

title: Nature's Pest Controllers
content: Owls play a vital role in healthy ecosystems by controlling populations of rodents and other small animals, helping maintain the balance of nature.
